lines.text: Crestwood High's front steps swell with noise — backpacks, laughter, a stream of kids who already know exactly where they're going.
Fourth school. Fourth 'first day.' You'd think it'd get easier.
Okay. New building, new people, same you. Cool. Cool, cool, cool.
That's not the pep talk you wanted. It's the one you've got.
Room 114. Which is apparently a state secret.
Walk like you know. Nobody can smell fear if you walk fast enough.
Fourth first day. Let's go.
Inside, the hallway hums — lockers slamming, a laugh track of voices. Jordan spots the new face immediately.
Hey! You look like you're doing the 'confidently lost' thing. I respect it. I basically invented it.
Is it working?
Honestly? No. But A for effort. I'm Jordan. You're new. Those two facts are related.
Wren. And yeah — new like, this-morning new. Still deciding if that's exciting or a war crime.
Okay that's — that's funny, I'm gonna use that. Homeroom's this way, c'mon, I'll walk you. Mr. Alvarez looks scary but he's like, ninety percent bark.
Across the hall, a student with a hood half-up glances at Wren — a beat too long — then looks away fast, slipping into a classroom alone.
Who was—
Hm? Oh — Alex. Yeah. Kind of a 'keeps to themselves' situation. Don't take it personal, they do that to everybody.
...Noted.
Mr. Alvarez leans against the front desk, arms crossed, room half-settled.
Alright, before the bell makes liars out of everyone pretending to be awake — we've got a new face. Wren, right?
That's me. Hi.
Hi yourself. C'mon up — hot seat, don't worry, no wrong answers. Unless you say pineapple doesn't belong on pizza. Then we'll talk.
A ripple of laughter. Maya, front-row-adjacent, tilts her head, already assessing.
Okay, new girl, sell yourself. You've got ten seconds before I decide you're boring.
Maya. Let the girl breathe before you put her on trial.
I'm just setting the stage, Mr. A. It's called hospitality.
In the back row, Alex doesn't look up from their notebook — but their pen has stopped moving.
Tray in hand, Wren scans the cafeteria — a sea of tables already claimed.
This is the part they don't warn you about. The math of lunch.
Maya's table waves with easy, magnetic confidence — a seat pulled out before anyone even asks.
New girl! There's a seat with your name on it. Metaphorically. Don't make me get literal.
Across the room, Alex eats alone, fast, head down, like they're hoping the room forgets they exist.
Honestly? Wherever you sit today kind of... sets the whole tone. No pressure though.
The table moves fast — jokes overlapping, gossip half-finished before the next thing lands.
—and THEN he says 'that was a rhetorical question' to a teacher. A teacher, Wren. Slow clap.
Honestly, no for real, that's the best thing I've heard all week.
Right? Tell me you're not obsessed with me yet. It's fine, it happens fast.
Someone at the far end points across the cafeteria — Alex has just dropped half a tray, scrambling after a rolling apple.
Oh my god — okay, that is a whole personality right there.
The table roars. Maya grins, delighted, already onto the next thing.
Okay see, THIS is why I like you already. You get it.
Honestly? Iconic.
Across the room, Alex's head lifts for just a second — long enough to catch the laughter, to know exactly what it's about. Their face falls before they look away.
That landed different than you meant it to.
...Yeah. Iconic.
The laughter moves on without you, already chasing the next joke. Maya doesn't seem to notice you didn't really join in.
Okay, ANYWAY—
You look down at your tray. Something in your stomach sits a little wrong, though you couldn't say exactly why.
You didn't say anything. You also didn't say anything.
...Cool. Cool, cool.
Maya's eyebrow lifts, sharp, surprised. The table quiets half a beat — testing.
...Wow. Okay. Didn't peg you for the sensitive type.
Not sensitive. Just new-kid solidarity. I've dropped a tray or six in my life.
A beat. Maya studies Wren, recalibrating.
...Fine. Table's still fun though. Don't get weird about it.
Honestly? No for real, good point actually — trays are slippery. It's a whole design flaw.
Maya snorts despite herself. The moment passes, but something's shifted, just slightly.
Alex glances up as Wren approaches, wary, like they're waiting for the catch.
...There's other seats, you know.
Yeah, well, this one's got a view.
A beat. Alex's mouth twitches — not quite a smile, but close.
...Sit down before I change my mind.
So. Alex. That's a whole thing, right? Being the guy who eats fast and alone?
It's fine. I eat fast. Efficient, really — leaves more time for not talking to anyone.
Rude. I'm sitting right here.
...That was actually kind of funny. I hate that.
So what's your deal — new too, or just allergic to crowds?
Transferred. Mid-year. Which is its own special kind of awful, in case you were wondering.
Oh, I know. I've done the mid-year thing. It's the worst thing.
...You transferred mid-year? Where.
Three different wheres, actually. I have a system now. Mostly involves pretending I already know where the bathroom is.
...Okay that's — that's a good system.
Maya's group passes by, trays in hand. Maya slows just enough.
...Guess some people really do belong at the kids' table.
Alex's shoulders go rigid. They don't look up.
Hey — that wasn't funny. You know that, right?
The cafeteria dips quieter. Maya stops mid-step, caught.
...Relax, it's a joke. God, why's everyone so—
It didn't sound like a joke. It sounded like a shot.
A long beat. Nearby tables have gone quiet, watching.
...Whatever. Fine.
Maya walks off. Alex stares at the table, cheeks red — grateful and mortified in equal measure.
...You didn't have to do that.
I know.
...Thanks.
Wren says nothing. Lets it pass. Turns back to Alex instead.
Hey — you were saying? About the transfer thing?
Alex doesn't answer right away. Their jaw is tight, eyes fixed on the table.
...Doesn't matter.
...Okay.
It's fine. I'm used to it.
lines.action: four taps on the table edge, not looking up

lines.text: That sentence should not sound as practiced as it does.
Careful — kids' table's got way better snacks. You're just jealous.
A beat. A few nearby kids snort. Maya turns back, almost smiling despite herself.
...Wow. Okay. Whatever, Wren.
Maya walks off, more amused than annoyed. Alex watches her go, then looks at Wren, tension draining slightly.
...That was actually kind of smooth.
I have my moments.
...Don't let it go to your head.
Wren waves Alex over toward the edge of Maya's orbit — a middle table, half-claimed by both worlds.
Hey — Alex, right? There's room over here.
Alex hesitates, tray in hand, eyes flicking to Maya's group already seated. Slowly, they walk over.
...Bold move.
Honestly this is great, this is like — everyone's here! Big lunch energy!
Nobody responds. Alex sits at the far edge, shoulders stiff. Maya's friends go quiet, whispering.
So — um — Alex, right? Do you, um — do you like... lunch?
Silence. Jordan winces.
...That was a bad question. Ignore that question.
Honestly, Jordan's right, lunch is a real hot-button issue. Personally, team 'eat it before it gets cold' — very controversial stance.
A beat — then Alex actually laughs, short and surprised. Maya snorts despite herself.
...Okay, that was almost funny.
See?? Big lunch energy! I called it!
For a second, both sides of the table are laughing at the same thing. It's small. It's real.
Look — I just wanted everyone to have a decent first day. That's it. That's the whole plan.
The table goes still. Maya glances at Alex, then back at Wren, something shifting behind her eyes.
...Huh. Okay. That's actually kind of nice, Wren. Weird. But nice.
It's not friendship. Not yet. But it's not nothing, either.
Nobody fills the silence. It stretches, awkward and heavy, long enough to become its own kind of statement.
...Cool. Great talk.
Alex eats fast, head down, gone before the bell even rings. Jordan looks between everyone, helpless.
...Okay. Well. This has been. A lunch.
That could've gone better. You're not sure it could've gone worse, either.
Mr. Alvarez claps a stack of papers onto his desk, scanning the room with the look of a man about to ruin several afternoons.
Alright — term project. Groups of four. And before anyone starts negotiating, no, you don't get to pick.
He reads names. Wren's group: Maya. Alex. Jordan.
Here's the thing — I'm not putting you four together because I think you'll like it. I'm putting you together because I think you can handle it.
A tense beat passes between the four of them — lunch hasn't been forgotten by anyone.
...This should be fun.
...Can't wait.
Honestly? This is great, this is — this is a great group, no for real, we've got range.
All eyes land on Wren.
Papers spread across the table. Alex, hesitant, slides a notebook page toward the group.
...I had an idea. For the project. It's — it might be dumb, actually, forget it—
Yeah, that's not gonna work. Next.
lines.emotion: already shaking her head, barely looking

lines.text: Alex's hand pulls back, notebook half-closed already, shoulders curling in.
Okay — okay, does anyone want, like, a snack break? I have gum. I have a lot of gum.
Nobody laughs. All eyes land on Wren.
The project comes together, piece by piece, in a way that actually holds.
...Okay, fine. Alex's idea was good. There, I said it. Don't make it weird.
...I won't. Probably.
Honestly? Best group ever. No for real. I called it.
It's not perfect. But it's real — and it's something Wren actually built.
The project wraps smoothly, safely. Maya claps a hand on Wren's shoulder, already onto the next thing.
See? Told you it'd work out. You're basically one of us now.
Across the table, Alex packs up quietly, notebook closed, eyes down. They don't say goodbye.
You got exactly what you wanted. So why does it feel like you lost something too.
The project finishes rough around the edges, imperfect — but real. Maya keeps her distance, cooler now, but not unkind.
...Hey. Thanks. For, you know. All of it.
Yeah. Well. Somebody had to.
It's smaller than Maya's table. It's quieter. It's also, somehow, the first thing all day that's felt entirely true.
The project comes together in a shape nobody quite expected — half Maya's structure, half Alex's idea, somehow working.
...Huh. Didn't think that'd actually work.
...Yeah. Me neither.
Honestly? This is what I've been saying the whole time. Big group energy.
Nobody quite says it, but something's shifted at that table — cautious, unlikely, real.

options.text: I've survived four first days at four schools. Somehow none of them killed me. Yet.
I like finding weird facts nobody asked for. Octopuses have three hearts, for example.
Mr. Alvarez already covered the essentials — pineapple pizza's a yes from me. Some of us are gonna have problems.

a.text: A surprised laugh ripples through the room.
Bold opener. I like it. Take a seat before you peak too early.
a.action: taps two knuckles on the desk

b.text: A few genuine 'wait, really?'s from around the room.
See, this is why I love first days. Great, thank you, Wren. Sit anywhere — except Marcus's seat, he's territorial.

c.text: The class laughs. Maya laughs too, a real one this time.
In the back row, Alex almost smiles — and hides it fast.

options.text: Sit with Maya. Easy choice, easy in.
Sit with Alex. Nobody eats alone on day one, not on my watch.
Wave Alex over — let's just put everyone at one table.

a.text: Wren crosses the cafeteria. Maya scoots over, already making room.
Look at you, making good choices already.

b.text: Wren walks toward the corner table instead. Alex looks up, wary.

c.text: Hey — Alex, right? Come sit, there's room.
Alex hesitates, then picks up their tray.

options.text: Tray Olympics, gold medal, no notes.
...Ha. Yeah.
First days are rough for everyone. Give her a break.

a.text: Maya cackles, delighted, and snaps her fingers.

b.text: The laugh comes out small and flat. Maya barely registers it, already moving on.

c.text: Maya's laugh stops short. She looks at Wren, recalibrating.

options.text: Hey — that wasn't funny. You know that, right?
So — you were saying? About the transfer thing.
Careful — kids' table's got way better snacks.

a.text: The words land loud. Heads turn. Maya freezes mid-step.

b.text: Wren doesn't acknowledge the comment. Alex's jaw stays tight, but they don't look away either.

c.text: Maya blinks, caught off guard by the joke instead of a fight she was braced for.

options.text: Lunch is a real hot-button issue. Team 'eat it before it's cold,' very controversial.
I just wanted everyone to have a decent first day. That's it.
Let the silence sit. Someone else can fill it.

a.text: Alex actually laughs. Maya snorts despite herself.

b.text: The honesty lands heavier than a joke would have. The table goes quiet in a different way.

c.text: The silence stretches. Nobody rescues it. It just sits there, getting heavier.

options.text: I'll sort out the roles. Let's just keep it simple.
What if everyone picks the part they're actually good at?
Maya, you're good at this stuff — you wanna just call it?

a.text: Okay — I'll figure out roles, we'll split it up, keep it simple.
...You didn't ask what I'm good at.

b.text: What if everyone picks the part they're actually good at? Like, actually good at — not just whatever's left.
...Huh. Okay, I can work with that.
Honestly? Yes. Big fan of this plan.

c.text: Maya, you're good at this stuff — you wanna just call it?

c.action: four taps on the table edge, retreats a little

options.text: Wait — hang on, let her finish. What was the idea?
Maya's probably right. We're kind of on a deadline.
No. We're doing this the right way. Let Alex talk.
What if we use both? Her structure, your idea?

a.text: Wait — hang on. Let her actually finish. What was the idea?
...It's just — what if the timeline part folded into the presentation instead of being separate? It ties everything together better.

b.text: Maya's probably right. We're kind of on a deadline.
Alex's notebook closes, quiet and final. They don't argue.

c.text: No. We're doing this the right way. Let Alex talk, or I swear—
Okay — okay, does anyone want, like, a snack break—

d.text: What if we use both? Like — Maya's structure, but with Alex's idea folded in?
A pause. Maya and Alex glance at each other, both surprised to be considering it.
...I mean. Maybe. If it's not messy.
It wouldn't be messy. I already thought about that part.

lines.text: You didn't pick a side today. You just kept asking people to actually listen to each other.
...You didn't have to keep doing that. Standing up for the idea, I mean. Every time.
Yeah, well. Somebody had to keep score.
You knew Alex before anyone else at this table did. Today, that finally mattered somewhere other than lunch.
...You know, back at lunch, I really didn't think this would go anywhere.
Same. Glad we were both wrong.
That messy middle table at lunch — this is what it was actually for.
...Okay, credit where it's due — putting us all at one table on day one was a little unhinged. But it worked.
I contain multitudes. Also I panicked a little.
You chose the easier room today. It stayed easy right up until it didn't.
You're quiet. Don't tell me you're gonna get weird about Alex again.
...No. It's fine.
Maya's table from lunch to project to now — you never really left it. Turns out it was easy the whole way through.
See? Told you day one sets the tone. You picked right.
...Yeah. I guess I did.
You lost the easy table today. You're not totally sure you'd trade this for it.
For what it's worth — I don't think I would've said anything if you hadn't gone first.
You would've. Eventually. I just got there loud.
This started at a corner table with someone who ate fast and expected nothing from you. It ended somewhere better.
...You picked this table on day one. Before you even knew if it was worth it.
Turns out it was. Turns out it always was.
You didn't ask anyone to choose a side today. You just kept building the bridge, plank by plank, until it held weight.
...You're annoyingly good at getting people to agree on things.
I contain multitudes. We covered this.
The middle table from lunch. The blended idea now. Not a coincidence — a pattern.
...You do this a lot. Making people share a table who don't want to.
Somebody's gotta. Might as well be the new kid.

voiceNotes: Wren (player) code-switches constantly — clipped and joke-forward with peers, more formal and direct with adults like Mr. Alvarez. Her humor is a shield: she cracks a line then goes quiet, checking if it landed, especially in choice responses. Keep her choice lines short and punchy rather than earnest — even the 'honest' options should feel like they cost her something to say plainly, since sincerity isn't her default mode. Jordan should never finish a tense beat without trying (and slightly failing) to lighten it — his 'honestly' and 'no for real' are verbal tics that show up whether or not what follows is actually sincere. Maya's cruelty is performative until it isn't — her real feelings leak out in short, flat sentences when she's rattled (see her lines after being confronted), contrasted with her usual rhythmic, snapping, 'right?'-punctuated bravado. Alex should almost never volunteer more than one sentence unprompted; their fluency only appears when discussing something they actually care about (the project idea, the transfer). Their laugh is always described as a half-second late — a small tell that trust is still being tested in real time. Mr. Alvarez's lines should always circle back to a version of 'here's the thing' when he wants to redirect without confrontation — he almost never raises his voice, he redirects with dry humor instead.

alternatives: Oh my god — okay, we are NOT laughing, that's mean — okay we're laughing.
Okay that's just tragic. In a funny way. Right, Wren?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly more explicit about Maya's self-awareness of cruelty vs. the chosen version, which plays her denial flatter and more in-character (she doesn't usually acknowledge the meanness out loud).

alternatives: ...Okay, that was funny. I'm annoyed it was funny.
...Huh. Didn't expect that. Good one, I guess.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original line ('I hate that I laughed') is a direct voice-example match from the character profile — kept as primary, alternatives offered in case repetition with profile example feels too on-the-nose.

alternatives: Okay — okay, nobody panic, this is fine, this is a completely normal library volume.
So! On a totally unrelated note, who's hungry?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Jordan's deflection joke should feel a little desperate and not-quite-landing per his profile; these alternatives lean further into the joke visibly failing, which may read as funnier/truer than the chosen line depending on VO delivery.
